briefing.opening: Good morning/afternoon. Welcome. We will now begin the climate briefing for March 27, 2026.

sections.title: 🔴 Deepening Climate Crisis and Retreating Responses
sections.content: Recent reports simultaneously reveal a retreat in climate action and a deepening impact of the climate crisis. Notably, news that South Korea and Japan are reactivating coal power generation due to the Iran war starkly illustrates how geopolitical conflicts can undermine climate change response efforts [1]. In Illinois, USA, a surge in home insurance premiums is being observed due to climate change [43], and the importance of indigenous agriculture is being highlighted amidst concerns that modern agriculture is collapsing due to climate change [45]. Furthermore, Arctic sea ice has recorded its lowest level for the second consecutive year [68], and research linking record outbreaks of tropical diseases to extreme weather caused by climate change [52] warns of the global severity of the climate crisis. In addition, an analysis indicating that the UK is halving its climate finance support to developing countries [70] raises concerns about responsible climate action from the international community.

sections.title: 🇰🇷 Domestic Climate Change Response and Carbon Neutrality Efforts
sections.content: Domestically, multifaceted efforts are continuing to address climate change and accelerate carbon neutrality. National fisheries research institutions gathered in Yeosu, Jeollanam-do, to discuss climate change response measures [2], and Jeonbuk Self-Governing Province confirmed its 2026 implementation plan for agriculture and rural climate change response, demonstrating its commitment to accelerating carbon neutrality [3]. The South Chungcheong Provincial Council, centered around its Special Committee on Carbon Neutrality, is intensively reviewing the implementation status of the '2045 Carbon Neutrality' goal, showcasing a responsible stance at the local government level [12, 19]. Furthermore, the government is promoting national health policies that link youth health promotion with climate crisis response [8, 10], and Incheon Dong-gu and Gwangju Seo-gu plan to operate 'Visiting Environmental Classrooms' and carbon-neutral environmental education starting in 2026 to encourage citizen participation [13, 14]. The Central Regional Forest Service's seedling sharing event [15] and Gwangmyeong 7-dong Residents' Autonomy Council's 'Carbon-Neutral Plant Anabada' event [20] are contributing to spreading a culture of active practice among local communities and citizens.

sections.title: 🌱 Accelerating Renewable Energy Transition
sections.content: The transition to renewable energy is being addressed as a crucial agenda both domestically and internationally, with a particular focus in Korea on housing support projects and the establishment of self-sufficient models at the regional level. Various local governments, including Gwangju City, Jeonbuk Province, Daegu City, Gangneung City, and Gimpo City, are promoting new and renewable energy housing support projects starting in 2026, providing subsidies for the installation of solar photovoltaic, solar thermal, and geothermal energy sources to enhance energy self-sufficiency [23, 28, 32, 33, 34, 35, 36, 39, 41]. Haenam County in Jeollanam-do officially launched a public-private consultative body for building a renewable energy self-sufficient city, marking a full-scale start to region-led energy transition [24, 25, 26, 27, 31, 37, 38, 42]. Concurrently, discussions on the designation of the Taean Offshore Wind Power Complex [40] and the necessity of enacting a law to foster the renewable energy generation equipment industry [30] highlight the importance of expanding large-scale renewable energy infrastructure and strengthening the industrial ecosystem. However, the Yeongdeok wind power plant fire incident [29] calls for deep reflection on safety management during the renewable energy transition process and the issue of 'outsourcing risk'.

sections.title: 🌍 International Community's Climate Response Discussions and Challenges
sections.content: The international community is discussing various aspects of climate change and exploring response measures. Scientists are conducting dive training under the polar ice in the Arctic and Antarctic, directly investigating the effects of warming [59, 61, 65]. Meanwhile, the criticism that San Francisco's climate change goals do not account for the artificial intelligence (AI) boom [44] suggests the need for proactive responses to the increased energy demand that new technological advancements will bring. The Maryland Supreme Court's dismissal of a climate change damages lawsuit by local governments [48] continues the discussion on the complexity of climate litigation and the locus of legal responsibility. On a positive note, CARICOM is advancing a common position on climate change issues [62], and India has reaffirmed its commitment to climate action by announcing new Paris Agreement pledges to reduce greenhouse gas emission intensity [69]. Additionally, indigenous representative Hindou Oumarou Ibrahim emphasized the role of indigenous peoples in resolving the climate crisis, underscoring the importance of a comprehensive approach [54].

briefing.closing: Today's briefing has highlighted the complex facets of the climate crisis and the diverse domestic and international efforts to address it. Continued attention and cooperation will determine our climate future. This concludes today's climate briefing.
